section_snippets.game_plan: A functional Treasure deck normally starts by establishing the infrastructure that makes the strategy real. In the opening hand, The Dragon’s Touch should look for lands, ramp, early enablers, and at least one card that shows the deck can move toward token material. Hands that contain only expensive payoffs may look exciting, but they often indicate a payoff-heavy build that will stall before the strategy matters. Hands with only setup and no conversion point should be flagged more gently, because many Commander engines need a few turns before their payoffs appear. During the early game, the deck wants to place its commander or first engine piece on the table without becoming the obvious arc
section_snippets.commander_identity: Commanders fit Treasure in several different ways. Engine commanders create the relevant resource or trigger repeatedly, which means The Dragon’s Touch should raise commander-support confidence even if the 99 has only medium density. Payoff commanders do not always create the resource themselves, but they make every supporting card better. Enabler commanders reduce costs, provide access, grant keywords, or make the first engine piece reliable. Color-access commanders are weaker evidence: they may allow the deck to play the strategy, but they do not prove the deck is built around it. section_snippets.role_buckets: Core card roles for Treasure should be evaluated as a system. The Dragon’s Touch should not count all support cards equally. A repeatable enabler is usually more important than a one-shot enabler. A payoff that wins or stabilizes is more important than a payoff that only adds extra value while ahead. A glue card that connects two packages can be more important than a famous staple if it keeps the actual engine functioning. Common role categories for this strategy include: - commander synergy pieces. - repeatable enablers. - payoffs that convert the plan into advantage. - card draw tied to the plan. - interaction that does not interrupt the plan. - protection for the main engine. - finishers
section_snippets.synergy: The Dragon’s Touch should recognize Treasure through clustered evidence rather than isolated card tags. A single payoff, one narrow enabler, or a random staple should not define the deck. The strategy becomes more credible when multiple markers point to the same plan and the commander either supports that plan directly or benefits from it indirectly. Important synergy markers: - commander text directly references the action or resource. - multiple cards share the same trigger pattern. - the deck contains both enablers and payoffs. - the win condition uses the generated resource rather than generic combat alone. - cut candidates are judged by role fit rather than raw power. - repeatable token
section_snippets.cut_logic: Cut logic for Treasure should rank cards by replaceability, not by raw power. The Dragon’s Touch should first protect the commander’s engine, scarce enablers, key payoffs, and cards that bridge supported themes. After those are protected, it should look for cards that duplicate a role inefficiently, fail to support the primary plan, or create resource conflicts. High-confidence cut signs: - The card has no commander synergy, no package synergy, and no essential generic role. - The card is a payoff with too few enablers. - The card is an enabler with no conversion point. - The card conflicts with Treasure's main resource or timing. - The card is only good when the deck is already ahead. - The
section_snippets.protect_logic: Protection logic for Treasure is about preserving the cards that make the deck recognizable and functional. The Dragon’s Touch should protect cards that directly enable the commander, create the main resource, convert that resource into advantage, or provide scarce support that the strategy cannot easily replace. Protection does not mean the card is untouchable forever; it means the card should not be cut by generic optimization logic without checking the structure around it. section_snippets.replacement_logic: Replacement logic for Treasure should recommend categories before exact cards unless the user has requested specific upgrades or provided collection data. This keeps The Dragon’s Touch useful for collection-first deck building and prevents it from suggesting cards that are unavailable, too expensive, outside the intended bracket, or wrong for the user’s preferred style. Useful replacement categories: - More commander synergy. - More engine density. - More card draw attached to the plan. - More protection. - More flexible interaction. - Cleaner win conditions. - Lower curve where the plan is too slow. - Better mana support. - More repeatable token production. - More token conversion payoffs.
section_snippets.power_level: Power level changes how Treasure should be judged. At casual tables, the strategy can afford more theme cards, pet cards, taplands, and slower engines as long as the deck has a real plan. At upgraded casual tables, the deck needs cleaner role balance, better interaction, and less reliance on unsupported payoffs. At high-power tables, the strategy needs efficient enablers, compact finishers, lower curve, stronger protection, and fewer cards that only work when opponents ignore the deck. Combo-adjacent tables require the tool to distinguish backup combo from true combo-primary construction. Hands that contain only expensive payoffs may look exciting, but they often indicate a payoff-heavy build that will stall before the strategy matters. Hands with only setup and no conversion point should be flagged more gently, because many Commander engines need a few turns before their payoffs appear. section_snippets.commander_identity: Commanders fit Artifact/Treasure Combo-Value in several different ways. Engine commanders create the relevant resource or trigger repeatedly, which means The Dragon’s Touch should raise commander-support confidence even if the 99 has only medium density. Payoff commanders do not always create the resource themselves, but they make every supporting card better. Enabler commanders reduce costs, provide access, grant keywords, or make the first engine piece reliable. Color-access commanders are weaker evidence: they may allow the deck to play the strategy, but they do not prove the deck is built around it. A glue card that connects two packages can be more important than a famous staple if it keeps the actual engine functioning. section_snippets.synergy: The Dragon’s Touch should recognize Artifact/Treasure Combo-Value through clustered evidence rather than isolated card tags. A single payoff, one narrow enabler, or a random staple should not define the deck. The strategy becomes more credible when multiple markers point to the same plan and the commander either supports that plan directly or benefits from it indirectly. section_snippets.cut_logic: Cut logic for Artifact/Treasure Combo-Value should rank cards by replaceability, not by raw power. The Dragon’s Touch should first protect the commander’s engine, scarce enablers, key payoffs, and cards that bridge supported themes. After those are protected, it should look for cards that duplicate a role inefficiently, fail to support the primary plan, or create resource conflicts. section_snippets.protect_logic: Protection logic for Artifact/Treasure Combo-Value is about preserving the cards that make the deck recognizable and functional. The Dragon’s Touch should protect cards that directly enable the commander, create the main resource, convert that resource into advantage, or provide scarce support that the strategy cannot easily replace. Protection does not mean the card is untouchable forever; it means the card should not be cut by generic optimization logic without checking the structure around it. section_snippets.replacement_logic: Replacement logic for Artifact/Treasure Combo-Value should recommend categories before exact cards unless the user has requested specific upgrades or provided collection data. This keeps The Dragon’s Touch useful for collection-first deck building and prevents it from suggesting cards that are unavailable, too expensive, outside the intended bracket, or wrong for the user’s preferred style. section_snippets.power_level: Power level changes how Artifact/Treasure Combo-Value should be judged. At casual tables, the strategy can afford more theme cards, pet cards, taplands, and slower engines as long as the deck has a real plan. At upgraded casual tables, the deck needs cleaner role balance, better interaction, and less reliance on unsupported payoffs. At high-power tables, the strategy needs efficient enablers, compact finishers, lower curve, stronger protection, and fewer cards that only work when opponents ignore the deck. Hands that contain only expensive payoffs may look exciting, but they often indicate a payoff-heavy build that will stall before the strategy matters. A repeatable enabler is usually more important than a one-shot enabler. A payoff that wins or stabilizes is more important than a payoff that only adds extra value while ahead. section_snippets.synergy: The Dragon’s Touch should recognize Commander-Created Landfall through clustered evidence rather than isolated card tags. A single payoff, one narrow enabler, or a random staple should not define the deck. The strategy becomes more credible when multiple markers point to the same plan and the commander either supports that plan directly or benefits from it indirectly. Important synergy markers: - commander text directly references the action or resource. - multiple cards share the same trigger pattern. - the deck contains both enablers and payoffs. - the win condition uses the generated resource rather than generic combat alone. - cut candidates are judged by role fit rather than raw power.

section_snippets.game_plan: A functional Clues / Investigate deck normally starts by establishing the infrastructure that makes the strategy real. In the opening hand, The Dragon’s Touch should look for lands, ramp, early enablers, and at least one card that shows the deck can move toward token material. Hands that contain only expensive payoffs may look exciting, but they often indicate a payoff-heavy build that will stall before the strategy matters. Hands with only setup and no conversion point should be flagged more gently, because many Commander engines need a few turns before their payoffs appear. During the early game, the deck wants to place its commander or first engine piece on the table without becoming the
section_snippets.commander_identity: Commanders fit Clues / Investigate in several different ways. Engine commanders create the relevant resource or trigger repeatedly, which means The Dragon’s Touch should raise commander-support confidence even if the 99 has only medium density. Payoff commanders do not always create the resource themselves, but they make every supporting card better. Enabler commanders reduce costs, provide access, grant keywords, or make the first engine piece reliable. Color-access commanders are weaker evidence: they may allow the deck to play the strategy, but they do not prove the deck is built around it. section_snippets.role_buckets: Core card roles for Clues / Investigate should be evaluated as a system. The Dragon’s Touch should not count all support cards equally. A repeatable enabler is usually more important than a one-shot enabler. A payoff that wins or stabilizes is more important than a payoff that only adds extra value while ahead. A glue card that connects two packages can be more important than a famous staple if it keeps the actual engine functioning. Common role categories for this strategy include: - commander synergy pieces. - repeatable enablers. - payoffs that convert the plan into advantage. - card draw tied to the plan. - interaction that does not interrupt the plan. - protection for the main engine. -
section_snippets.synergy: The Dragon’s Touch should recognize Clues / Investigate through clustered evidence rather than isolated card tags. A single payoff, one narrow enabler, or a random staple should not define the deck. The strategy becomes more credible when multiple markers point to the same plan and the commander either supports that plan directly or benefits from it indirectly. Important synergy markers: - commander text directly references the action or resource. - multiple cards share the same trigger pattern. - the deck contains both enablers and payoffs. - the win condition uses the generated resource rather than generic combat alone. - cut candidates are judged by role fit rather than raw power. - repea
section_snippets.cut_logic: Cut logic for Clues / Investigate should rank cards by replaceability, not by raw power. The Dragon’s Touch should first protect the commander’s engine, scarce enablers, key payoffs, and cards that bridge supported themes. After those are protected, it should look for cards that duplicate a role inefficiently, fail to support the primary plan, or create resource conflicts. High-confidence cut signs: - The card has no commander synergy, no package synergy, and no essential generic role. - The card is a payoff with too few enablers. - The card is an enabler with no conversion point. - The card conflicts with Clues / Investigate's main resource or timing. - The card is only good when the deck i
section_snippets.protect_logic: Protection logic for Clues / Investigate is about preserving the cards that make the deck recognizable and functional. The Dragon’s Touch should protect cards that directly enable the commander, create the main resource, convert that resource into advantage, or provide scarce support that the strategy cannot easily replace. Protection does not mean the card is untouchable forever; it means the card should not be cut by generic optimization logic without checking the structure around it. Protect these categories first: - Commander support cards that repeatedly enable Clues / Investigate. - Main engine pieces that create, copy, replay, sacrifice, cast, or convert the strategy’s resource. - Key
section_snippets.replacement_logic: Replacement logic for Clues / Investigate should recommend categories before exact cards unless the user has requested specific upgrades or provided collection data. This keeps The Dragon’s Touch useful for collection-first deck building and prevents it from suggesting cards that are unavailable, too expensive, outside the intended bracket, or wrong for the user’s preferred style. Useful replacement categories: - More commander synergy. - More engine density. - More card draw attached to the plan. - More protection. - More flexible interaction. - Cleaner win conditions. - Lower curve where the plan is too slow. - Better mana support. - More repeatable token production. - More token conversio
section_snippets.power_level: Power level changes how Clues / Investigate should be judged. At casual tables, the strategy can afford more theme cards, pet cards, taplands, and slower engines as long as the deck has a real plan. At upgraded casual tables, the deck needs cleaner role balance, better interaction, and less reliance on unsupported payoffs. At high-power tables, the strategy needs efficient enablers, compact finishers, lower curve, stronger protection, and fewer cards that only work when opponents ignore the deck. Combo-adjacent tables require the tool to distinguish backup combo from true combo-primary construction. What makes Clues / Investigate fair is usually visible setup, interactable permanents, combat
